content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 17, 2009

Mr. Edwin "Thorpe" Romero
Chairman, Barona Band of Mission Indians
1095 Barona Road
Lakeside, California 92040-1599

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-09-044

Dear Mr. Romero:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift and campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your letter seeks general
information, we are providing you informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Will the Barona Tribe's awarding of education grants to schools, where each
grant application is accompanied by a letter of endorsement from a state legislator, result
in a reportable gift from the Barona Tribe to the state legislators who submit letters of
endorsement?

CONCLUSION

Under the Act, the Barona Tribe's awarding of the education grants will not result
in reportable contributions or gifts from the Barona Tribe to state legislators who submit
letters of endorsement on behalf of the schools receiving the grants. A state legislator
who submits an endorsement letter for a school that is the recipient of a grant of $5,000
or more will be required to file a "behested payment" report under Section
82015(b)(2)(B)(iii).

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-09-044
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Barona Band of Mission Indians ("Barona Tribe") provides education grants
to public and private schools to assist the schools in purchasing much-needed supplies
and materials, such as books and computers.

Under the Barona Tribe's Education Grant Program, a school may apply for a
$5,000 grant to be used for materials that promote academic improvement, including
books, computers, and laptops. Grant funds may not be used for personnel, wages, sports
equipment, athletic or recreational equipment, giveaway books, office furniture or
supplies. Each grant application must be accompanied by a letter of endorsement from a
state legislator. A school may receive one grant per year; and only one grant per year
may be awarded per legislator. The Barona Tribe invites the legislator who provides the
endorsement letter for a school that is awarded a grant to participate in the presentation of
the check to the school. The Barona Tribe provides no tangible benefits, such as food or
drink, to the legislator as part of the presentation of the check.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines a "contribution" as "a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a
payment of a debt by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except
to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." (Section 82015.)

Regulation 18215 further defines the term "contribution" as follows:

"(a) A contribution is any payment made for political purposes for
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. A
payment is made for political purposes if it is:
(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action
of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or
candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure; or
(2) Received by or made at the behest of the following or any agent
thereof:
(A) A candidate;
(B) A controlled committee;
(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state central
committee, county central committee, assembly district committee or any
subcommittee of such committee; or
(D) An organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes,
including, but not limited to, a political action committee established by
any membership organization, labor union or corporation . . .."
(Emphasis added.)

File No. 1-09-044
Page No. 3

In addition, the Act's contribution limits provide that no person may make a
contribution to a state legislator of more than $3,900 per election. (Section 85301.)
Accordingly, the Barona Tribe seeks advice as to whether the education grants it provides
to schools at the behest of legislators will constitute contributions under the Act.

As cited above, the Act states that a "contribution" includes a payment made at
the behest of a candidate.³ (Section 82015(b).) A payment is made at the "behest" of a
candidate whenever it is made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the
express, prior consent of a candidate. (Section 82015; Regulation 18225.7.) Section
82015(b)(2) sets forth several exceptions to the general rule that a payment made at the
behest of a candidate is a contribution. Specifically, Section 82015(b)(2) provides that a
behested payment is not a contribution under the following circumstances:

"(A) Full and adequate consideration is received from the candidate.
"(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was
made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective office.
The following types of payments are presumed to be for purposes
unrelated to a candidate's candidacy for elective office:
"(i) A payment made principally for personal purposes, in which case it
may be considered a gift under the provisions of Section 82028. Payments
that are otherwise subject to the limits of Section 86203 are presumed to
be principally for personal purposes.
"(ii) A payment made by a state, local, or federal governmental agency
or by a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
"(iii) A payment not covered by clause (i), made principally for
legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes, in which case it is
neither a gift nor a contribution . . .."

Here, the payments from the Barona Tribe to the schools are clearly made "at the
behest" of a state legislator, as each grant application must be accompanied by a letter of
endorsement from a state legislator. However, the grants fall within the exception to the
definition of contributions and gifts under Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii) for certain
payments made at the behest of a candidate that are unrelated to his or her candidacy for
elective office and "principally for legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes."
Accordingly, the education grants provided to schools by the Barona Tribe with the
endorsement of legislators do not result in reportable contributions or gifts by the Barona
Tribe to the state legislators under the Act.

In connection with the education grant program, The Barona Tribe may wish to
advise the legislators that they will be required to file a behested payment report

³ Under the Act, an elected officer retains his or her status as a candidate until he or she
terminates that status as provided in Section 84214 and Regulations 18404 and 18404.1. (Section 82007.)

File No. I-09-044
Page No. 4

concerning a grant of $5,000 or more to a school. Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii), copy
enclosed, provides that, even if a payment made at the behest of an elected officer is not
a contribution to that officer, the officer must file a report describing the payment. The
"behested payment report" must be filed with the officer's agency within 30 days of the
date of the payment. The report shall contain the following information: name of payor,
address of payor, amount of the payment, date or dates the payment or payments were
made, the name and address of the payee, a brief description of the goods or services
provided or purchased, if any, and a description of the specific purpose or event for
which the payment or payments were made. A legislator who recommended a school
receiving a grant would file a behested payment report with the Senate or the Assembly.
For state agencies, a copy of the report is forwarded to the Commission and information
concerning behested payments is posted on the Commission's website.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
HPW:jgl

Enclosure

who submits an endorsement letter for a school that is the recipient of a grant of $5,000
or more will be required to file a "behested payment" report under Section
8201s(bx2)(BXiii).

File No. l-09-044
